coredata.dc:title: Competition of the roles of π-conjugated domain between emission center and quenching origin in the photoluminescence of carbon dots depending on the interparticle separation
coredata.dc:creator: Yoo, Hyo Jeong
Kwak, Byeong Eun
Kim, Do Hyun
coredata.dc:description: The π-conjugated domain, which is one of frequently-suggested fluorescence origin of carbon dots (CDs), can also act as fluorescence quenching origin in aggregated state. This dual role of the π-conjugated domain in CDs offers a challenge to prediction of photoluminescence (PL) characteristics in solid-state. Here, we focus on the effect of π-conjugated domain on the PL phenomenon for different interparticle separation using three types of CDs with different degree of carbonization. The degree of carbonization was controlled by selecting three alcoholic solvent species for solvothermal reaction, which can influence carbonization of CDs. In dilute dispersion, CDs from three different solvents possessed two emissive centers originated from surface- and core-state with different relative intensities depending on the degree of carbonization. With the increase in concentration of CDs, the higher contents of π-conjugated domain changed the dominant PL center from surface- to core-state due to the greater reduction of surface-derived emission. In aggregated state, the emission from surface-state was totally suppressed, and the π-conjugated domain contents influenced the emission efficiency of core-state. Based on PL decay results, we proposed the PL mechanism of the CDs for different interparticle separation, which would extend the boundary in the interpretation of PL properties of CDs.

head.ce:title: Competition of the roles of π-conjugated domain between emission center and quenching origin in the photoluminescence of carbon dots depending on the interparticle separation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon, 34141, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon, 34141, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The π-conjugated domain, which is one of frequently-suggested fluorescence origin of carbon dots (CDs), can also act as fluorescence quenching origin in aggregated state. This dual role of the π-conjugated domain in CDs offers a challenge to prediction of photoluminescence (PL) characteristics in solid-state. Here, we focus on the effect of π-conjugated domain on the PL phenomenon for different interparticle separation using three types of CDs with different degree of carbonization. The degree of carbonization was controlled by selecting three alcoholic solvent species for solvothermal reaction, which can influence carbonization of CDs. In dilute dispersion, CDs from three different solvents possessed two emissive centers originated from surface- and core-state with different relative intensities depending on the degree of carbonization. With the increase in concentration of CDs, the higher contents of π-conjugated domain changed the dominant PL center from surface- to core-state due to the greater reduction of surface-derived emission. In aggregated state, the emission from surface-state was totally suppressed, and the π-conjugated domain contents influenced the emission efficiency of core-state. Based on PL decay results, we proposed the PL mechanism of the CDs for different interparticle separation, which would extend the boundary in the interpretation of PL properties of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), emerging fluorescent carbon materials, have recently gained extensive attention due to their fascinating merits like low toxicity [1,2], simple preparation [3,4], plentiful precursors [1,3,5], strong luminescence [4,6], color tunability [1,7–9], easy functionalization [10], and high photostability [11]. Despite the complicated emission phenomenon due to their structure [12–14], the active studies on photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of CDs have enabled us to tune their PL properties [12,15–18]. However, most interpretation on the PL mechanism of CDs is based on dilute dispersion-system, while the luminescence of CDs significantly depends on the interparticle separation, which affects the PL intensity [6], peak position of emission band [19–21], excitation-dependency [22], and relative PL contribution of each emissive state [23]. Thus, we should understand the PL phenomenon of CDs in concentrated-dispersion or solid-state where their emission mechanism is substantially different from dilute dispersion-state [19,23,24].

So far, three types of structural parts including carbogenic core [25–27], functional groups [26,27], and molecular fluorophores [17,19] have been considered as main emissive origins of CDs. Among them, the core-state, which is derived from π-conjugated domain containing other dopant elements, was suggested for the main PL center in dispersion system [25–29]. However, several studies on the possibility of π-conjugated domains to work as quenching origin of CDs in aggregated state were reported [21,30,31]. They claimed that for the quenching-resistant CDs, formation of π-conjugated structure, which accepts energy at a short interparticle distance, should be avoided during the synthesis to minimize resonance energy transfer (RET) and reduction of quantum yield (QY). It seems conflicting to the argument that the π-conjugated domains act as emission center. Thus, it is essential to understand the role of π-conjugated domains in their PL phenomenon for various interparticle separation to analyze the emission characteristics of CDs both in dispersion- and solid-state.

To isolate the effect of π-conjugated domains on the PL phenomenon from other PL origins, the formation process should be precisely controlled to regulate only the π-conjugated domain content maintaining other structural features such as size and type of surface group. The formation process of CDs can be regulated by a variety of factors including temperature [19,27,29], reaction time [32–34], and solvent species [35–38] during the synthesis. Among them, the change of solvent species can give the different structure to CDs [39,40] by providing different reaction conditions in terms of vapor pressure [41], polarity [41], or solubility of precursors [42–44], which determines the concentration of precursors. Depending on the solvent, only the specific reaction can be allowed reducing the possible side reactions [45,46], controlling the formation of CDs more precisely.

In this work, we investigate the variation of PL properties depending on the interparticle distance using three CDs with different contents of π-conjugated domains. We regulate the carbonization process by selecting three different alcoholic solvent species with similar chemical structures during solvothermal reaction, while introducing the same type of functional group to three CDs. Using the CDs, we study the effect of π-conjugated carbon contents on the relative PL contribution change of each emissive center during the reduction of interparticle separation. Finally, through transient PL decay analysis, we would unveil the PL mechanism of each emissive center in reduced interparticle distance by investigating the influence of π-conjugated carbon contents on the recombination process of each emissive state. It would open the avenue to interpret PL characteristics of core-state in CDs in various practical applications such as light-emitting-diodes, energy conversion, sensing, and fluorescent ink.

Citric acid, urea, methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and ethyl acetate were of ACS reagent grade. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) solution was of 32 wt% concentration dissolved in water. All of the chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and were used without further refinement.

Citric acid (1 g), urea (2 g), and 10 mL of MeOH were introduced in 15 mL Teflon reactor. Then, the reactor was placed in an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After that, the autoclave was cooled at room temperature for 2 h, and brown dispersion of CDs was obtained. To remove non-luminescent large particles, the dispersion of the CDs was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. Then, we carefully collected the supernatant dispersion of the CDs leaving the precipitated large particles. To wash the product, the collected dispersion of the CDs and 25 mL of ethyl acetate were introduced in 50 mL conical tube. The mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, leading to precipitation of the CDs. After removing the yellow supernatant dispersion, the CDs was rinsed by 30 mL of fresh ethyl acetate and centrifuged again at 8000 rpm for 10 min. We repeated this washing step twice for purification of the CDs. After removing the supernatant solution, we dried the precipitated CDs in a vacuum oven at 25 °C for 24 h. Finally, we obtained yellowish powder, which is referred as m-CDs. Additionally, we synthesized two different CDs by replacing MeOH with EtOH and IPA. For simple description, we refer the EtOH- and IPA-based CDs as e-CDs and i-CDs, respectively. All of the synthesis procedures for e-CDs and i-CDs are the same with m-CDs, except the reaction solvent. All types of CDs were well dispersed in water due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on their surface, which is beneficial to further characterization of CDs in dispersion.

To investigate the PL properties of the CDs, the UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer LAMBDA 1050 UV–Vis/NIR spectrometer. Steady-state PL maps and absolute quantum yield (QY) of dispersion of the CDs were obtained using HORIBA Fluoromax-4C. To acquire the PL maps, PL intensity of CDs in a range from 350 to 750 nm was measured at excitation wavelength from 300 to 500 nm with an interval of 5 nm for dilute dispersion and from 300 to 600 nm with an interval of 10 nm for concentrated dispersion and solid-state, followed by plotting by OriginPro 2019 program. Absolute QY in solid-state was measured using a Hamamatsu Quantaurus-QY absolute PL QY measurement spectrometer. For structural analysis of the CDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using the FEI Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin field emission TEM operated at 300 kV, and particle sizes were measured using Image J software. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were obtained using a RIGAKU D/MAX-2500 multi-purpose thin-film X-ray diffractometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) data were collected using a TG209 F1 Libra with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on Agilent 400 MHz 54 mm NMR DD2. The attenuated total reflectance (ATR) spectra were acquired using a Thermo Scientific Instrument Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer with built-in all-reflective diamond ATR module. Simply, the powder samples were located on a sampling stage and fixed by a pressure tower, followed by measurements using OMINC software. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using a Thermo VG Scientific Kalpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscope. Elemental analysis was performed using Thermo Scientific FLASH 2000 series. Raman spectra were acquired using HORIBA LabRAM HR Evolution Visible-NIR Raman spectrometer. Zeta-potential of the CDs was measured using an Otsuka ELS-Z2. pH value of dispersion of the CDs was measured using Orion™ Versa Star Pro™ Multiparameter Meter with 8302BNUMD pH electrode. Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) analysis was performed using an Edinburgh Instruments FL920 fluorescence lifetime spectrometer.

We prepared three types of CDs by solvothermal reaction of citric acid and urea using three different alcoholic solvents which are methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA). For simple description, the three CDs were referred as m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs for MeOH-, EtOH-, and IPA-based CDs, respectively. In Fig. 1 a–c, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of all the three CDs showed well-dispersed spherical particles with the similar average size of 3.10, 3.12, and 3.09 nm for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively, where the size was measured using Image J software. The size distributions of three CDs were fitted well to Gaussian distribution, and the mean values from the fitted curve were 3.03, 3.10, and 2.96 nm for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively, showing negligible difference in the calculated average sizes. The lattice spacing was measured as 0.246 nm for all the three CDs, corresponding to the d-spacing of graphitic lattice structure [47]. In X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, all the three CDs showed distinct peaks around 27° corresponding to graphitic (002) facet (Fig. S1), which demonstrates successful formation of CDs. The intensity of graphitic peak decreased in the order of m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs indicating decrease in the degree of carbonization. Raman spectra of three CDs were also acquired at various excitation wavelengths, but no reliable peak was obtained due to strong PL interference (Fig. S2). From thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in Fig. 1d, we observed the most rapid thermal decomposition of i-CDs, followed by e-CDs and m-CDs, resulting in the different amount of residual mass at 750 °C measured as 38.9, 35.2, and 32.2% for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively. It means that m-CDs possess the largest amount of rigid structure such as π-conjugated domains, while i-CDs have the most amorphous structure, which may be predominantly comprised of polymeric chain [23]. In the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. 1e), the peaks appearing from 0.7 to 1.7 ppm correspond to the protons attached to aliphatic carbon, and the region around 2.3–2.7 ppm represents protons attached to benzylic groups [48–50]. The small peaks at 5.75–5.85 and 7.15 ppm correspond to protons attached to vinyl groups and aromatic rings, respectively [48–50]. From the comparison, we can see that the relative peak intensity of benzylic groups, vinyl groups, and aromatic groups decreased compared to aliphatic carbon groups in the order of m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, which indicates the different degree of graphitization. In fact, the broad peak at 5.6 ppm for i-CDs shows protons attached to residual amine groups originating from urea [48].

Detailed chemical structure of the CDs was investigated by attenuated total reflectance (ATR). As shown in Fig. 1f, the ATR spectra of three CDs were similar to one another, indicating that they possess almost the same types of surface functional groups. Specifically, in the ATR spectra of three CDs, we could observe the stretching vibrations of N–H2 at 3429 and 3334 cm−1 and C–N at 1448 cm−1, which were derived from urea [48]. Also, a broad peak of O–H stretching around 3200 cm−1 and absorption shoulder of CO at 1723 cm−1 arising from citric acid were detected [48]. The absorption shoulder of C–H and distinct peaks of CN stretching, CC stretching, and –CH3 bending appeared at 2775, 1665, 1580, and 1334 cm−1, respectively [48], which may be formed during carbonization. Although CO in aromatic ketone can also show absorption band at 1665 cm−1, its content in CDs would be insignificant considering that the removal of heteroatoms is followed by the formation of aromatic domains [51]. Meanwhile, in the order of m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, the absorption from N–H2 and C–N groups increased, and the absorption from C–H and –CH3 groups decreased.

To reveal the influence of solvent species on formation process of the CDs, we analyzed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of three CDs. In Fig. S3a-c, the full XPS spectra indicated the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms in all the three CDs with the peaks at 285, 399, and 531 eV, respectively [8,10,27]. With the increase in the number of methyl groups in the solvent structure, the atomic ratios of carbon decreased, while the heteroatom content increased, which is consistent with the results of other structural analysis. In Fig. S3d, the C1s spectrum of m-CDs was comprised of four peaks representing C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, CO/CN, and COOH bindings at 284.5, 286.0, 287.5, and 288.3 eV [8,10,27]. In Fig. S3d-f, three C1s spectra shared the same constituents of chemical bonding, but showed different portion of each component. The portion of C–C/CC binding, which is related to the carbogenic structure of CDs, was estimated as 60.6 to 55.6 and 51.0% for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively. Inversely, the portion of carbon-heteroatom bindings such as C–O/C–N, CO/CN, and COOH was highest for i-CDs followed by e-CDs and m-CDs, which compose polymeric structure or surface functional groups in CDs. It means that with the addition of methyl groups on the alcoholic solvent species, the CDs possessed larger amount of heteroatom containing linkages. In Fig. S3g, the N1s scan consisted of C–NC, N–H, and N-(C)3 with the peak positions of 398.8, 399.5, and 400.7 eV, respectively [10,52]. Consistent with the ATR result, the N1s spectrum of i-CDs showed the highest content of N–H bindings, while the lowest content of N–H bindings was observed in m-CDs (Fig. S3g-i). In Fig. S3j-l, the O1s scan displayed the highest content of CO binding at 531.1 eV compared to that of C–O binding at 533.0 eV [10,27] for i-CDs followed by e-CDs and m-CDs. Combined with N1s scan results, it implies the least consumption of N–H and C–O bindings during i-CDs synthesis followed by e-CDs, and m-CDs.

For further characterization of the core-structure, we additionally performed structural analysis of the residual product of three CDs after TGA. In elemental analysis, the carbon content of three CDs increased from ∼40 to ∼70% after TGA (Fig. S4), which demonstrates the significant removal of heteroatoms during the thermal decomposition of the CDs. The residual products of all the CDs after TGA showed similar atomic contents, while the pristine CDs showed the decrease of carbon content and increase of nitrogen content in the order of m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs. XPS survey scan spectra also support that the atomic percent of carbon and nitrogen was similar for three CDs after thermal decomposition (Fig. S5a-c). For C1s scan spectra, most of carbon atoms form C–C/CC binding, while the portion of heteroatom-binding carbon was insignificant (Fig. S5d-f). We could observe only N-(C)3 and C–NC bindings in N1s scan, and their compositions were also similar for three CDs (Fig. S5g-i), which implies that nitrogen-doping content in π-domain would be comparable for three CDs. The discrepancy in the atomic contents between elemental analysis and XPS results would arise from the different analytic methods of the two tools. In Fig. S6, the residual product of three CDs displayed analogous Raman spectra with similar G- and D band intensities. Combined with the TGA and XPS results, it implies that all the CDs possess the comparable amount of defects in sp2 carbon domain due to similar graphitic nitrogen content. However, the amount of π-conjugated domain would be different as demonstrated in XRD and TGA of the pristine CDs (Fig. S1 and Fig. 1d).

In general, CDs are formed by coupling of small molecular precursors through dehydration or condensation [16,53]. Such polycondensation leads to the formation of longer molecular chains or cross-linked structure, which is defined as polymerization step [16,19,53]. Combined with the other structural analysis, the XPS results of the pristine CDs imply the lowest degree of cross-linking between citric acid and urea during the synthesis of i-CDs followed by e-CDs and m-CDs. In addition, under such high temperature condition, the elimination of heteroatoms including nitrogen and oxygen occurs, and partial carbonization results in the growth of sp2 domains [16,53]. In this case, the reaction using three different solvents resulted in the different π-conjugated carbon content in the CDs; the most carbonized features for m-CDs and the least carbonized features for i-CDs. This trend is consistent over repeated experiments (Fig. S7). The structural difference would be derived from the consistent trend in physicochemical properties of three alcoholic solvents (See supporting information and Table S1). Solvent has an impact on the kinetics or equilibrium during reaction by governing the reaction atmosphere in terms of polarity, solubility, vapor pressure, and other characteristics [41]. For the alcoholic solvents, solubility, polarity, and vapor pressure increase with the decrease in the number of alkyl groups, which would facilitate the carbonization process by reducing the activation energy [41]. Experimentally, the effect of solubility was demonstrated by comparing m-CDs and the CDs based on MeOH with different volumes of 4, and 13 mL, which are referred as m-CDs-4 and m-CDs-13, respectively. As shown in Fig. S8 and S9, the residual mass after TGA decreased from 40.64 to 35.36%, and the graphitic peak intensity decreased and became broader in the order of m-CDs-13, m-CDs, and m-CDs-4, indicating the reduction of sp2 domain content in the CDs with the decrease in solvent volume. It would be due to the increase in the soluble quantities of precursors with the increase in the volume of solvent, which may result in the increase of active precursors to participate reaction influencing the degree of carbonization. Still, however, other characteristics including vapor pressure and polarity also can be other factors that influence the reaction leading to the structural difference between the CDs since reaction pressure should be linked to the efficiency of carbonization due to the gas release, and polarity also have an impact on the activation energy of condensation and carbonization processes.

Except the degree of carbonization, ATR results indicate little difference in the formation of specific functional groups depending on the solvent species. It means that we precisely controlled the sp2 domain contents only without changing the types of chemical functional groups in the prepared CDs, as expected. Actually, when we compared the structural features and PL properties of the CDs which were synthesized by following the same procedures except using other solvent species such as N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), acetone, water, dimethyl surfoxide (DMSO), and formamide, they possessed significantly different structural features in terms of functional group types as well as the content of crystalline part, showing markedly different PL features from one another (Fig. S10 and S11). It implies that those solvent species had significantly different impact on the formation of structure and resultant PL characteristics of the CDs since the non-alcoholic solvents possess totally different physicochemical properties due to their different chemical structures. Meanwhile, since ethylene glycol (EG) possesses two hydroxyl groups, it can be considered as a solvent for the comparison with the alcoholic solvents. Therefore, we additionally prepared CDs using EG, and compared their structure with those of e-CDs. As shown in TGA result (Fig. S12a), the residual mass of EG-based CDs (EG-CDs) after thermal decomposition was 23.16%, and their graphitic peak intensity in XRD pattern was weaker than that of e-CDs (Fig. S12b and S11), which indicates less carbonized feature of EG-CDs compared to e-CDs. In ATR spectrum, the dominant functional groups in EG-CDs were significantly different from e-CDs showing strong absorption peaks at 3273 and 1038-1086 cm−1 which correspond to O–H stretching and C–O stretching, respectively (Fig. S12c). The two strong absorption bands from C–O would arise from phenyl alcohol and ether groups [48]. Although XPS survey scan showed that the EG-CDs are composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen like e-CDs, narrow scan data indicate that relative portion of C–O binding in EG-CDs was higher than that of C–O binding in e-CDs, while the content of COOH binding in EG-CDs was lower in comparison with e-CDs (Fig. S13). Although EG shares the similar chemical structure with ethanol, this reveals that structural feature of EG-CDs was significantly different from e-CDs, which would be due to the presence of multiple functional groups in EG. In other words, there is a possibility for EG to be incorporated into the structure of CDs as reported in other previous works [54,55]. Like in those works, EG may act as a precursor, making EG-CDs to possess abundant hydroxyl groups rather than carboxylic acid- and amine groups which are dominant functional groups in critic acid and urea, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL properties of CDs in dilute dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: The π-conjugated domains have been frequently suggested as one of main emissive origins of CDs being referred as core-state in dilute-dispersion system. Recently, however, reports on the solid-state luminescence of CDs proposed that π-conjugated domains can cause the self-quenching phenomenon [21,30,31], regarding the core structure as quenching origin. Based on these two different arguments, to fully understand the effect of π-conjugated domains on the PL properties of CDs, we employed the CDs with different π-conjugated carbon contents, and investigated the change of their PL characteristics depending on interparticle separation. We firstly analyzed the PL properties of individual CDs under dilute dispersion condition. Fig. 2 a shows the schematics of the synthetic route and the emission characteristics of the three CDs dispersed in water with the low concentration of 0.05 mg/mL. It is interesting to see that the CDs exhibited gradual enhancement of green emission in the order of i-CDs, e-CDs, and m-CDs under the excitation wavelengths of both 365 nm and 405 nm. To investigate their absorption properties, we obtained UV–vis spectra of three CDs. In Fig. 2b, the UV–vis spectra of three CDs showed similar absorbance over the whole range from 200 to 600 nm, which implies that all the three CDs possess similar transition centers. The absorption peak and shoulder at 237 nm and 275 nm arise from π-π∗ transition of CC and CN bonds in core [24,36,56], respectively, which are scarcely related to the fluorescence of CDs [36]. The strong absorption around 350 nm originates from n-π∗ transition in heteroatom-containing groups [24,36,56]. In lower energy region above 400 nm, all three spectra showed an absorption shoulder at 425 nm originating from nitrogen-doped π-conjugated domains [29,57], and its intensity was reduced gradually in the order of m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs. It indicates the different contents of nitrogen-doped π-conjugated domains in the CDs as demonstrated in structural analysis.

The emission properties of three CDs were also investigated by recording PL maps. The three CDs were dispersed in water with the same concentration of 0.05 mg/mL. In Fig. 2c–e, all the three CDs possessed two different emissive centers around 450 nm and 525 nm, displaying blue and green emissions. We referred the blue emissive center as center 1 and green emissive center as center 2, respectively. When we compared the three PL maps, m-CDs showed the highest relative PL intensity of center 2 compared to center 1, while i-CDs showed the lowest relative PL intensity of center 2. This implies that by changing the solvent species for reaction, we would control the construction of center 2, showing gradual change in the relative intensity with respect to center 1. The PL difference due to structural difference among three types of CDs was also consistent over repeated experiments (Fig. S14). Meanwhile, the PL features of three CDs were totally different from that of the CDs based on other solvents including EG (Fig. S15) due to the structural difference derived from different reaction environment. The quantum yield (QY) of center 1 was measured as 9.18, 13.34, and 14.05% for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively. For center 2, the QY was 8.69, 10.00, 9.08% for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively, showing comparable values one another.

Since there is strong relationship between the structural features and PL properties of CDs, the discrepancy in the relative PL intensity of two centers for three CDs must be linked to the content of sp2 domains. As the emission from center 2 was enhanced with the increase of π-conjugated carbon content in the CDs, it is reasonable to assume that center 2 was derived from sp2-hybridized core structure. To verify this, we recorded PL maps for three CDs under acidic condition because the emissive center associated with sp2-hybridized core structure is less influenced by external species compared to other PL origins [58]. As can be seen in Fig. 3 a–c, compared to neutral condition, all the three CDs showed the enhanced relative PL intensity of center 2 to that of center 1 under acidic condition. Comparison of three PL maps displayed the similar trend to neutral condition in that the relative PL intensity of center 2 to that of center 1 increased for the CDs with higher content of π-conjugated domain. For the detailed analysis, we compared the pH sensitivity of both emissive centers. As the optimum excitation wavelength was around 360 nm for center 1 and 420 nm for center 2 (Fig. S16), respectively, we selected those wavelengths as representative excitation wavelengths for each center. Fig. 3d shows the relative PL intensity of three CDs under acidic condition (pH3) compared to neutral condition (pH7) (IpH3/IpH7) at the excitation wavelengths of 360 and 420 nm. The PL intensity excited at 360 nm, which corresponds to center 1, was significantly reduced under acidic condition by 75.3, 65.0, and 51.7% for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively, compared to neutral condition. On the other hand, PL intensity under 420 nm excitation, which is related to center 2, was nearly preserved at pH3 by 96.3, 90.4, and 85.9% of for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively. Also, whereas the slight red-shift of PL band was observed for center 1, center 2 showed no change in PL center (Fig. S17). Generally, it is known that these quenching and red-shift of emission at acidic condition are originated from the protonation of surface functional groups [8,59–65]. In particular, addition of hydrogen changes surface charge of CDs and their energy structure, and consequently, the protonated functional groups would act as non-radiative channel, resulting in fluorescence quenching under acidic condition. For center 1, both PL intensity and peak position were sensitive to pH change like surface-states, while those of center 2 were less influenced by solvent environmental change corresponding to core-like feature. Actually, when we recorded the PL intensity change with pH value under high energy excitation (ex. 280 nm), which should be attributed to π-π∗ transition of core-state [56,58], the PL intensity was reduced to around 90%, showing similar decrease to center 2 (Fig. S18).

We also tested the photo-stability of both emissive centers under UV-irradiation using m-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3e, center 1 was almost disappeared showing dramatically reduced PL intensity to around 10%, while the PL intensity from center 2 was preserved over 60% after irradiation time of 2 h. It shows that center 2 has similar photo-stability with core-derived emission (Fig. S19), implying that center 2 arises from rigid structure such as sp2-hybridized carbon core [15,56,66,67]. In fact, in Fig. S20a, before the irradiation, the emission was contributed dominantly by center 1 when excited at 360 nm, but with the increase of UV exposure time, the emission from center 1 diminished, and PL contribution from center 2 became dominant, indicating high photo-stability of center 2. On the other hand, when excited at 420 nm, the dominant PL center was maintained at 526 nm, displaying similar trend to core region excited at 280 nm (Fig. S20b and c). Therefore, the spectroscopic analysis results demonstrated that the center 2 is originated from sp2-hybridized carbon core structure, which is consistent with the structural investigation.

The possible emission origins of the remaining center 1 are surface functional group and molecular fluorophore. To identify the emission origin of center 1, we performed dialysis of the three CDs in water and compared the PL maps of dialyzed CDs with that of dialysate. As can be seen in Fig. S21, the PL maps of dialyzed CDs and their dialysate were similar to each other for all the three CDs. It implies that the emissive origin of center 1 cannot be isolated from the CDs through dialysis, and thus the center 1 does not originate from fluorescent molecules, but rather from surface-states. As a result, we can conclude that the CDs possess two main PL centers, showing blue emission from surface groups and green emission from core structure in dilute dispersion system. Although core-state in CDs usually possess higher energy than surface-states, longer wavelength emission from core-state than that from surface-state is also possible through both the extension of sp2 conjugated system [27,28] and the nitrogen doping [57,68,69]. Moreover, since the various surface moieties in our CDs are possible candidates for blue emission origin [26,70–72], the CDs could possess larger energy gap for surface-states than that for core-state as reported in the previous studies [22,73].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL properties of CDs in concentrated dispersion or in solid-state
ce:section.ce:para: Since the change of the roles of π-conjugated domains arises from the interparticle distance variation, we investigated PL change of m-CDs using different concentrations of 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 4 a, we could clearly observe the presence of two emissive centers at 450 and 525 nm for m-CDs in dispersion with the concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. However, during the increase in concentration of m-CDs to 0.5 mg/mL, center 2 became dominant PL center (Fig. 4b and c), which is opposite to the dilute dispersion case in Fig. 2c. For the concentration of 1 mg/mL, we identified the presence of only center 2 as can be seen in Fig. 4d. This indicates that with the increase in concentration of m-CDs, the dominant PL center was shifted from surface- to core-states. In the case of e-CDs and i-CDs, we also observed the same trend in the change of PL characteristics as concentration of the CDs increased, which made center 2 as dominant PL feature (Fig. S22). Meanwhile, the weakening of center 1 contribution was different for three CDs during the same concentration change. It indicates that the π-conjugated domain content determines the sensitivity of the PL characteristics to the reduction of interparticle separation.

For quantitative comparison, we plotted their maximum PL intensity ratios between the two emissive centers (IMax, center2/IMax, center1) as a function of the concentration of CDs at the fixed emission position at 450 and 525 nm for center 1 and center 2, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 5 a, m-CDs displayed the greater increase of IMax, center2/IMax, center1 with the increase of concentration from 0.01 to 0.5 mg/mL than e-CDs and i-CDs. Actually, when IMax, center2/IMax, center1 was fitted as a function of concentration of CDs in log scale, we obtained the different slope of 4.8, 4.3, and 2.9 for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively. Meanwhile, the values of IMax, center2/IMax, center1 for m-CDs was always highest among three CDs for all concentration followed by e-CDs and i-CDs. It would be due to the difference in the relative amount of two emissive origins arising from the different π-conjugated domain contents for three CDs. Here, we should clarify whether the increase of IMax, center2/IMax, center1 was mainly derived from reduction of IMax, center1 or the increase of IMax, center2. When we plotted IMax, center1 and IMax, center2 separately, IMax, center1 showed dramatic decrease, while IMax, center2 was relatively constant with the increase of concentration (Fig. 5b and c). In other words, the higher content of π-conjugated domain in the CDs caused greater suppression of center 1 emission during the reduction of interparticle separation than the emission of center 2. It is consistent with the structure model of CDs where the core exists in the inner part, while surface groups exist on the surface of CDs [58]. At this point, it is worthy to note that the quenching of surface states was not caused by direct contact of CDs, which does not agree with the aggregation-caused-quenching model suggested in the previous studies [6,74,75].

To understand how the PL characteristics of CDs are modified by the change of interparticle distance, we should clarify the quenching mechanism of center 1. Therefore, we investigated the PL spectra of dispersions of three CDs with high concentration of 1 mg/mL for the emission characteristics under high energy excitation. As shown in Fig. S23, three CDs displayed green emission around 525 nm under high energy excitation from 300 to 380 nm which corresponds to the excitation energy for blue emission in dilute dispersion. It means that during the reduction of interparticle distance of CDs, the emission from surface-state was vanished, by transferring the excited energy of center 1 to center 2. Since the absorption shoulder around 433 nm, which corresponds to center 2, is strongly overlapped with the emission band of center 1 located at 450 nm as shown in Fig. 2, it is possible for center 2 to accept the energy emitted from center 1. Generally, there are quenching phenomena occurring with substantial spectral overlap between absorption and emission spectra including Dexter transfer, resonance energy transfer (RET), and re-absorption [76]. However, those phenomena occur in different range of interparticle distance [76]. Thus, we calculated the mean interparticle distance of the CDs with the concentration change to distinguish the possible photo-physical phenomena. By assuming that the CDs do not form aggregates based on their high zeta-potential values (Table S2), the mean interparticle distance from center to center was estimated as shown in Table S3 (See supporting information) [77]. With the increase of concentration from 0.01 to 1 mg/mL, the interparticle distance was reduced from ∼62 to ∼13 nm. Considering that the mean particle diameter was ∼3 nm, the average distance from surface to surface would range from ∼59 to ∼10 nm in the concentration range of 0.01–1 mg/mL. Since most of molecular quenching phenomena including Dexter transfer occur effectively at a very short interparticle distance of several angstrom scale [76], the disappearance of center 1 would be mainly due to RET or re-absorption. Thus, center 2 may accept energy from center 1 either by radiative- or non-radiative process as commonly observed in various types of CDs [30,78–81]. Exciton-self trapping, which occurs by the stabilization of trap states due to the interaction between CDs and solvent molecules, is another possible mechanism to explain the quenching of surface-states [82], but it cannot be adopted in the distance-dependent PL quenching of the CDs since solvent polarity and strength of hydrogen bonding do not change upon the decrease of interparticle separation.

To discuss the PL phenomenon of the CDs in solid-state, we studied the PL maps of three CDs in powder form. As shown in Fig. S24, only center 2 existed in solid-state, and the high energy excitation from 300 nm to 380 nm was linked to 525 nm emission similar to concentrated dispersion case. Also, there was noticeable difference in the degree of fluorescence quenching for three CDs, showing the different QY values of 0.3, 0.9, and 5.0% for m-CDs, e-CDs, and i-CDs, respectively, in solid-state. In other words, m-CDs showed most quenched fluorescence in solid-state followed by e-CDs and i-CDs as shown in Fig. S25. It may be due to the different content of π-conjugated domain in the CDs, because the crystalline sp2 carbon domains work as a self-quenching origin [19,20,30,31].

For further confirmation of the aforementioned PL phenomenon, we performed time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) for three CDs. As can be seen in Fig. 6 a and Table S4, all the CDs possessed the same number of lifetime components for center 1, and the average lifetimes for three CDs were similar one another with the value of ∼9.33 ns under low concentration of 0.05 mg/mL, which indicates that three CDs share the same types of PL origins for center 1. Similarly, the average lifetimes of center 2 showed negligible difference among three CDs with the value of ∼6.40 ns under low concentration of 0.05 mg/mL, implying that they also share the same PL origin for center 2 (Fig. 6b, Table S5). Although the size of π-domain can influence the PL decay characteristics, the noticeable difference of PL lifetime is observed when the π-domain size varies considerably [83]. Actually, Lee et al. observed the continuous increase of PL lifetime of single-crystalline graphene quantum dots from 3.48 to 5.12 ns with the increase of π-conjugated domain size from 10 to 90 nm [83]. Since three CDs showed similar average sizes of ∼3 nm as described in TEM images (Fig. 1a–c), it is fair to display analogous PL decay curves for center 2 showing similar PL lifetime values in dilute dispersion (Fig. 6b). Meanwhile, when the concentration of CDs was increased to 1 mg/mL, the average lifetime of center 1 was reduced by ∼1 ns for all the three CDs (Fig. 6c, Table S6). It represents the increased non-radiative relaxation of excited electron in center 1 during the reduction of interparticle separation [76,84]. On the other hand, the PL decay rates of center 2 remain almost constant for three CDs during the change in concentration of CDs from 0.05 to 1 mg/mL, implying that the decay of center 2 is less influenced by the reduction of interparticle distance (Fig. 6d, Table S7). This result supports the postulation that the center 1 acts as an energy donor, while the center 2 absorbs or accepts the transferred energy by RET process since re-absorption does not induce the lifetime change [85]. Also, when we compared the PL lifetime of center 2 excited at 375 nm, three CDs showed increased PL lifetime for center 2, recording the longest lifetime for m-CDs as 9.31 ns followed by e-CDs and i-CDs with the lifetime of 8.98 and 7.82 ns, respectively (Fig. 6e, Table S8). It is because the emission process of acceptor was delayed during RET [84,86], and the different length of lifetime for three CDs would be caused by the different RET efficiencies arising from different content of sp2 domain. According to Brus group, the energy of donor fluorophores can be transferred to single- and few-layer graphene by RET, and the efficiency of energy transfer was enhanced with the number of layers [87]. They claimed that since the interaction between graphene layers is weak, the individual graphene layer plays a role as an independent energy transfer channel. In our case, the increased degree of carbonization of the CDs led to the highly ordered stack of graphene layers as shown in XRD spectra (Fig. S1), which implies the increase in the number of energy transfer channels leading to different RET efficiencies. Actually, when we calculated the spectral overlap integral of three CDs (Fig. S26), m-CDs showed the highest overlapped region with the integral value of 1.384
×
1015 nm4/M cm followed by e-CDs and i-CDs with the integral values of 7.372
×
1014 nm4/M cm and 6.408
×
1014 nm4/M cm, respectively. It indicates the higher possibility of RET process between surface- and core-state with the increase of π-conjugated domain content [30,76,84].

In solid-state, the lifetime of center 2 decreased showing the largest reduction of lifetime for m-CDs followed by e-CDs and i-CDs (Fig. 6f, Table S9). This is due to the generation of non-radiative decay channels induced by aggregation [9,76], which deactivates the excited state of center 2. The larger amount of π-conjugated domain in the CDs may greatly facilitate the formation of the aggregates due to the significant attractive force arising from strong π-π interactions [23,88,89], which leads to the larger number of aggregation-induced non-radiative decay channels [74]. It is consistent with the results of previous works that the larger amount of sp2 domain contents led to more effective deactivation or quenching of emission of the CDs in solid-state [19,20,30,31]. In other words, the core-states kept intact during the concentration change of the CDs, and only the direct contact of the CDs resulted in the deactivation of core-derived emission. Consequently, it indicates that the sp2 domains in carbogenic core work as main emissive sites in whole concentration range including solid-state, but also work as quenching origin for surface states and even for core-state depending on the interparticle distance range.

Fig. 7 summarizes the PL mechanism of the CDs for different interparticle distance range. When the CDs are in dilute-dispersion, they display strong blue emission from surface-states, which is designated as center 1. However, in the dispersion with high concentration of CDs, the excited energy of center 1 is transferred to center 2 by resonance with interparticle distance of several nanometer scale, leading to green-emission-dominant PL feature from core-state. On the other hand, we may observe the loss of fluorescence from center 2 in solid-state due to the formation of aggregation-induced non-radiative decay channel, which would be more effective as the increase of π-conjugated domain contents in the CDs. These results agree well with previously observed phenomena of various types of CDs [19,20,30,78–81], and the proposed mechanism would be applicable for many types of CDs which possess π-conjugated domain and surface-state. We believe that our study would extend the understanding of the role of π-conjugated domain in the CDs.

We investigated the change of PL phenomena depending on the interparticle separation using three types of CDs with different degree of carbonization. The CDs were prepared using different alcoholic solvents including MeOH, EtOH, and IPA with the same precursors. From TGA and ATR results, we confirmed that three CDs possessed different content of rigid structure such as sp2 conjugation system with the same types of surface functional groups. Combined with the XPS analysis, we revealed that the CDs had different degree of carbonization due to the different physicochemical properties of solvents. In dilute dispersion system, they displayed two emission centers at 450 and 525 nm showing both blue- and green emissions, and the relative intensity of green emission compared to blue emission was gradually weakened with the reduction of π-conjugated domain content. Combined with the results of PL intensity change with pH variation and UV-irradiation, we assigned π-conjugated core for green emission origin and surface-derived groups for blue emission origin, respectively. The comparison of PL maps revealed that the higher content of π-conjugated domain led to the greater suppression of surface-state emission under high concentration of the CDs, while PL from core-state was effectively quenched in solid-state as π-conjugated domain contents increased. From the PL lifetime analysis, we confirmed that the lifetime of surface-states decreased with increase of concentration of the CDs since the π-conjugated domains accepted the energy from surface states by non-radiative process. On the other hand, the core-derived emission was deactivated in solid-state due to the formation of aggregates, which was more effective with the increase of π-conjugated carbon contents. This study would contribute to the interpretation of the role of sp2 domains depending on the interparticle distance range, which will be essential for various applications of CDs.
